title: Megasthenes at the Wooden Capital
subtitle: When the Wooden Capital Faced the Western World
era: Greek embassy to Chandragupta's court, c. 300 BCE
region: Pataliputra, at the ancient Ganges-Son river system
moral: A stranger's account can illuminate history, but must be read with care.

sources.citation: Megasthenes, Indica, preserved in Arrian, Strabo, Diodorus, Pliny and Aelian
sources.note: Lost eyewitness account surviving only as later Greek and Roman fragments.

sources.citation: ASI excavations at Bulandibagh and Kumrahar, Patna (Waddell 1895; Spooner 1912-13; Jaiswal 1951-55)
sources.note: Wooden palisade beams and the 80-pillar hall evidence tied to ancient Pataliputra.

sources.citation: Richard Stoneman, The Greek Experience of India (Princeton University Press, 2019)
sources.note: Recent assessment of Greek knowledge and Megasthenes' reliability.

panels.shot: epic low-angle wide of city and river

dialogue.text: In the age of Chandragupta, when the Ganges met the Son, a Greek named Megasthenes walked the wooden capital and carried India’s image to a distant world.

panels.shot: bird’s-eye wide over caravan and river plain

dialogue.text: From the lands once ruled by Alexander, Megasthenes rode east through heat and dust, following rivers and roads into the heart of Chandragupta’s empire.

panels.shot: medium profile shot beside Indian guide on the road

dialogue.speaker: megasthenes
dialogue.text: How far yet to Palibothra, this wooden capital of yours?

panels.shot: wide establishing shot of confluence and city outline

dialogue.text: Where the Son once joined the Ganges, earth and water embraced a city. There, Pataliputra stretched like a long parallelogram along the river’s curve.

panels.shot: low-angle close-wide from moat up to wall and towers

dialogue.text: Megasthenes counted beams and bastions. A deep ditch, a timber wall with slits for archers, towers marching in line, and gates at measured intervals guarded the king’s capital.

panels.shot: architectural overhead diagram-like wide of wall section

dialogue.text: He would later tell the Greeks of a city girded with a palisade of timber, of hundreds of towers and dozens of gates, each placed with soldier’s care along the long walls.

panels.shot: medium at city gate from inside the ditch

dialogue.text: By treaty and diplomacy he came, bearing Seleukos’ words to Chandragupta. Even so, the gate captain read, weighed, and measured before he opened the way.

panels.shot: tracking wide shot as he enters through the gate

dialogue.speaker: megasthenes
dialogue.text: A city of wood, yet stronger than many of stone.

panels.shot: street-level wide shot of interior city avenue

dialogue.text: Within the palisade lay a living maze of streets and courtyards, storehouses and gardens, where clerks counted grain and soldiers drilled, and the pulse of an empire beat in dust and voices.

panels.shot: medium shot of Megasthenes sketching on tablet in a courtyard

dialogue.text: He drew Pataliputra in careful strokes. A city long, not round; flanked by rivers, ringed by defenses, ordered by royal will and the needs of war and grain.

panels.shot: majestic low-angle wide of palace exterior across water

dialogue.text: Beyond the common streets stood the king’s palace, its timber raised upon pillars, its halls set above pools and gardens. To Megasthenes, it rivalled the courts of Susa and Ecbatana.

panels.shot: interior wide of pillared hall from low angle

dialogue.text: He walked through a forest of pillars. Later ages would dig the earth here and find stone bases of such columns, silent witnesses to the halls he praised.

panels.shot: medium close-up on Megasthenes’ face as he looks up

dialogue.speaker: megasthenes
dialogue.text: This court is no barbarian tent, but a kingdom of law.

panels.shot: medium two-shot of Megasthenes before Mauryan official

dialogue.text: Between Seleukos and Chandragupta lay war once, then agreement. Now their men spoke of elephants and borders, of marriage ties and tribute, in the cool shadow of the timber palace.

panels.shot: nighttime wide shot of ramparts overlooking river

dialogue.text: He measured not only walls and halls, but vigilance. Watchfires burned along the ramparts; patrols paced; beyond, the river carried whispers of distant uplands and seas.

panels.shot: tight close-up on Megasthenes’ hand writing on a scroll

dialogue.text: In that wooden capital he began the work that would outlive him, shaping his Indica, trying to catch in words a land strange to his readers and larger than his sight.

panels.shot: montage-style wide showing imagined future Greek readers around a scroll

dialogue.text: His book would be copied, shortened, quarrelled over. Some called him careless; yet others, and our own age, sift his lines like dust to find grains of truth about Mauryan India.

panels.shot: archaeological bird’s-eye of excavation at Bulandibagh and Kumrahar

dialogue.text: When diggers at Bulandibagh unearthed buried palisade beams, when Kumrahar’s pillared hall rose again in plan, their spades brushed against the lines Megasthenes once wrote of towers, ditches and royal halls.

panels.shot: final wide shot of Megasthenes silhouetted on the wall at dawn

dialogue.text: So the Greek envoy on the wooden wall became a bridge of memory. His gaze, flawed yet priceless, lets us see Pataliputra’s strength, and warns us to weigh every stranger’s story with care.
